s: Where France shot its own soldiers for asking to be paid
w: African soldiers who survived Nazi prison camps only to be shot dead by the French Army on 1 December 1944. The Tirailleurs Senegalais at Thiaroye had been promised back pay; declassified orders suggest the killing was premeditated. Ousmane Sembene filmed Camp de Thiaroye about it -- France banned the film for a decade.
_facts: Thiaroye massacre occurred 1 December 1944
Victims were Tirailleurs Senegalais veterans of the 1940 Battle of France, recently freed from European prison camps
Between 35 and 300 killed; declassified French military documents show massacre was pre-planned
Ousmane Sembene directed Camp de Thiaroye (1988) depicting the events; film was banned in France for roughly a decade

s: The presidential villa of Senegal's poet-philosopher president
w: Senegal's first president and a founding theorist of Negritude spent his later years here on the Corniche Ouest. The villa is now a museum: his library, personal effects, correspondence -- the working space of a man who was simultaneously head of state and the first Black African elected to the Academie francaise.
_facts: Leopold Sedar Senghor (1906-2001) served as first president of Senegal 1960-1980
Senghor was a co-founder of the Negritude literary and philosophical movement in the 1930s
He was the first African elected as a member of the Academie francaise (1983)
The Corniche Ouest villa where he lived is now preserved as the Musee Leopold Sedar Senghor

s: Studios and workshops where Dakar's art world lives
w: A walled compound where dozens of painters, sculptors, and textile artists keep open studios. Dakar's Biennale -- Dak'Art, Africa's largest contemporary art event -- was launched partly to show international curators what was already happening in spaces like this. Show up unannounced; the gates are usually open.
_facts: The Village des Arts is a collective of artist studios in the Parcelles Assainies district of Dakar
Dak'Art (Dakar Biennale) has focused on Contemporary African Art since 1996 and is Africa's largest such event
The compound hosts painters, sculptors, weavers, and mixed-media artists in shared ateliers

s: Senegal's grandest stage, named for a Frenchman who loved Africa
w: Senegal's premier stage, named for Daniel Sorano, a French actor of the 1950s who championed African and colonial voices from his Parisian base. It opened in 1965, five years after independence, as the home of the Ballets Africains and the National Orchestra. Griots, playwrights, and mbalax stars have all worked this stage.
_facts: Theatre National Daniel Sorano located at 45 Avenue de la Republique, Dakar-Plateau
Named after Daniel Sorano (1920-1962), a French stage and film actor
Opened in 1965, five years after Senegalese independence
Home to the Ballets Africains, the National Orchestra, and diverse Senegalese performing arts

s: The independence square that used to honour a colonial enforcer
w: Known until 1960 as Place Protet, after a French governor-general who pacified the region by force. Renamed at independence on 4 April 1960, this was the flag-raising square. Dakar had sent delegates to the French parliament since 1848 under the Four Communes arrangement; this is where that colonial chapter closed.
_facts: Place Protet renamed Place de l'Independance when Senegal became independent on 4 April 1960
Named after Auguste Protet, French governor who enforced colonial rule in mid-19th century
Under the Four Communes arrangement, Dakar had sent representatives to the French Chamber of Deputies since 1848
The square was the site of the independence flag-raising ceremony

s: Lebou fishing beach where the catch becomes craft stalls by noon
w: At dawn the painted pirogues come in heavy with thiof and sardinella, women in grand boubous negotiating on the sand in Wolof. By mid-morning the same beach holds craft stalls: bronze, leather, baskets. One of the last working Lebou fishing beaches in Dakar -- the Lebou were here before France arrived.
_facts: Soumbedioune beach on the Corniche Ouest is one of Dakar's active Lebou fishing beaches
The Lebu are the indigenous ethnic group of the Cap-Vert peninsula, primarily a fishing community
The beach doubles as an artisan craft market selling bronzework, leather goods, and woven baskets
Thiof (Epinephelus aeneus, white grouper) is the prized local fish of Senegalese cuisine

s: The China-gifted concert hall at the heart of Dakar
w: A sweeping 1,800-seat hall, a gift from China inaugurated in 2010 -- the year Senegal marked 50 years of independence. It stands on the Medina's edge drawing ballet, opera, and mbalax spectaculars. The foyer is usually open; the ceiling height was designed to echo the scale of a giant baobab.
_facts: Grand Theatre National du Senegal was inaugurated in 2010, the year of Senegal's 50th independence anniversary
Constructed as a gift from China to Senegal
Seats approximately 1,800 people and hosts diverse performing arts including opera, ballet, and mbalax
Located on the edge of the Medina quarter

s: West Africa's first national art gallery, small and quietly essential
w: Senegal's national collection near Place de l'Independance. Founded in 1980, it holds works by the founding Senegalese modernists -- Iba Ndiaye, Papa Ibra Tall, Souleymane Keita -- whose paintings fused Negritude ideas with the West African palette. A small 1970s block that holds more than it looks.
_facts: Galerie Nationale d'Art du Senegal founded in 1980
Holds works by pioneering Senegalese artists including Iba Ndiaye, Papa Ibra Tall, and Souleymane Keita
Located on Rue Hassan II near Place de l'Independance in Dakar-Plateau
Senegalese School of art was influenced by Negritude philosophy promoted by President Senghor

s: The Moorish colonial courthouse where independence was tried
w: The 1954 colonial courthouse rises in a Franco-Moorish style -- arched loggias, white render, pale terracotta -- above the Plateau. France tried independence-era Senegalese political cases here before 1960; since then it has been the seat of Senegalese law. The iron gates stay open on weekdays.
_facts: Palais de Justice de Dakar built in 1954 during French colonial era
Designed in a Franco-Moorish architectural style with arched loggias
Hosted political trials of Senegalese nationalists before independence in 1960
Located on Rue Armand Angrand in the Rebeuss quarter of Dakar-Plateau

s: The city hall of an empire that called its citizens French
w: The Plateau city hall dates from the late colonial era when Dakar held an unusual legal status: the Four Communes arrangement gave its citizens nominal French citizenship from 1848 onward -- the strangest franchise in the empire. Now municipal offices; the shuttered colonial facade is intact.
_facts: Hotel de Ville (city hall) of the Plateau commune dates from French colonial era
Dakar was one of the Four Communes of French Senegal, whose inhabitants received nominal French citizenship from 1848
Blaise Diagne (1872-1934) was first full West African elected to the French Chamber of Deputies, representing the Four Communes
The building retains its original colonial facade with shuttered balconies

s: From Goree's Signares to today's activists, one museum
w: Conceived in 1987 and opened in 1994 under poet Annette Mbaye d'Erneville, the museum traces Senegalese women from pre-colonial queens and Signares -- the mixed-heritage businesswomen of Goree who accumulated property in the slave-trade era -- to present-day activists. Moved from Goree to the Corniche Ouest in 2015.
_facts: Museum conceived in 1987 by filmmaker Ousmane William Mbaye, opened in 1994
Opened under the direction of Senegalese writer Annette Mbaye d'Erneville
Originally located on Goree Island, moved to Place du Souvenir Africain et de la Diaspora on Corniche Ouest in May 2015
Signares were mixed-heritage African-European women who held significant property and status in Goree during the slave-trade era
